proposed_hypotheses.text: Higher values of feature_078 (continuous, range 30-90, mean ~65) are associated with longer pfs_months in the overall cohort.

analyses.result_summary: feature_078 has a strong positive linear association with pfs_months: each one-unit increase yields +0.174 months PFS (OLS, p<1e-300, R^2=0.73). Spearman rho=0.853, p<1e-300.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Higher levels of the 3-level ordinal feature_057 (0/1/2) are associated with shorter pfs_months; the level-2 group has the worst PFS.

analyses.result_summary: Mean PFS is 4.68 mo at feature_057=0, 3.49 mo at feature_057=1 (-1.19 mo vs 0, p<1e-300), and 2.38 mo at feature_057=2 (-2.30 mo vs 0, p<1e-300). Strong monotone decrease; feature_057 behaves like an adverse stage/severity indicator.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Higher feature_013 (continuous, right-skewed, range 0.12-3622) is associated with shorter pfs_months; effect is best modeled on the log scale.

analyses.result_summary: Each one-unit increase on log1p(feature_013) decreases PFS by 0.382 mo (p<1e-300). Spearman rho=-0.223. Behaves like an adverse biomarker (e.g., higher tumor burden / PSA-like).

proposed_hypotheses.text: Higher feature_006 (continuous 0-24.6) is associated with shorter pfs_months.

analyses.result_summary: Each one-unit increase in feature_006 reduces PFS by 0.075 mo (p~1e-225). Modest negative association.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Higher feature_009 (continuous 1.5-5.5) is associated with longer pfs_months.

analyses.result_summary: Each one-unit increase in feature_009 raises PFS by 0.495 mo (p~1e-166). Robust positive association.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Higher feature_067 (5-level ordinal, values 6-10 — pattern consistent with Gleason score) is associated with shorter pfs_months.

analyses.result_summary: feature_067 shows essentially no univariable association with PFS: coef=-0.006 mo per level, p=0.49. Group means are flat across 6/7/8/9/10 (3.71-3.76 mo). Does NOT behave like a pathologic grade in this cohort.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Mean pfs_months differs across racial/ethnic groups in feature_018 (asian, black, hispanic, white, other), with one or more groups showing systematically shorter PFS than others.

analyses.result_summary: Race-stratified mean PFS is essentially flat: asian 3.80, black 3.74, hispanic 3.74, other 3.75, white 3.74 mo. Range across groups = 0.06 mo. ANOVA F p=0.69 — no detectable racial/ethnic differences in PFS.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Mean pfs_months differs across insurance categories in feature_045 (medicaid, medicare, private, uninsured), with uninsured/medicaid patients showing shorter PFS than privately insured.

analyses.result_summary: Insurance-stratified mean PFS: medicaid 3.74, medicare 3.75, private 3.74, uninsured 3.73 mo. Range=0.03 mo. ANOVA F p=0.79 — no insurance-related differences in PFS.

proposed_hypotheses.text: After adjusting for the strong clinical predictors (feature_078, feature_057, log1p(feature_013), feature_006, feature_009, feature_067), race (feature_018) and insurance (feature_045) remain unassociated with pfs_months — i.e., there is no residual demographic disparity.

analyses.code: smf.ols('pfs_months ~ feature_078 + C(feature_057) + np.log1p(feature_013) + feature_006 + feature_009 + feature_067 + C(feature_018) + C(feature_045)', df).fit()
analyses.result_summary: All race indicators p>0.66 and all insurance indicators p>0.88 in the adjusted model (max |coef| ~0.003 mo). Adj R^2=0.969. Demographic factors confer no additional signal once clinical features are accounted for.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Among the 77 binary features, at least one binary indicator (likely a treatment/regimen flag) is associated with longer pfs_months after adjusting for the core clinical predictors.

analyses.code: for c in binary_cols: smf.ols('pfs_months ~ {clinical} + ' + c, df).fit() ; rank by p
analyses.result_summary: feature_109 (prevalence 10.0%) and feature_039 (prevalence 10.2%) emerge as the dominant binary predictors when added on top of clinical adjusters: feature_109 +0.152 mo (p~2e-183), feature_039 +0.149 mo (p~3e-177). All other binaries attenuate to |coef|<0.026 mo with p>1e-14.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_109 and feature_039 act synergistically: patients with BOTH features=1 have markedly longer PFS than would be expected from the sum of either alone (positive interaction term).

analyses.result_summary: When the 2-way interaction is fitted: feature_109 main coef ~0.001 (p=0.90), feature_039 main coef ~0.000 (p=0.94), interaction coef = +1.434 mo (p<1e-300, adj R^2=0.974). Cell means: 109=0,039=0 → 3.74 mo (n=40435); 109=1,039=0 → 3.67 (n=4467); 109=0,039=1 → 3.67 (n=4569); 109=1,039=1 → 5.22 mo (n=529). Either flag alone has NO effect on PFS; the benefit lives entirely in the combination.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The combination feature_109=1 AND feature_039=1 (call it combo+) is associated with longer pfs_months overall, with mean PFS ~1.5 months higher than combo-.

analyses.result_summary: Combo+ (n=529) mean PFS = 5.22 mo vs combo- (n=49,471) mean PFS = 3.74 mo (Δ=+1.49 mo, t=16.86, p~1e-63). Univariable OLS coefficient for combo: +1.488 mo.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Prevalence of the combo (feature_109=1 AND feature_039=1) differs across race (feature_018) — i.e., access to / receipt of this beneficial co-occurrence is unequal across groups.

analyses.result_summary: Combo prevalence by race: asian 0.99%, black 0.96%, hispanic 1.03%, other 0.82%, white 1.10%. Range across groups = 0.28 percentage points; chi-square test of independence p>>0.05. No meaningful disparity in receipt of the combo.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Prevalence of the combo differs across insurance (feature_045) — uninsured/medicaid patients receive it less often than privately insured.

analyses.result_summary: Combo prevalence: medicaid 1.10%, medicare 1.06%, private 1.02%, uninsured 1.20%. Range 0.18 pp; differences not significant. Insurance does not gate access to the combo.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The PFS benefit of the combo (feature_109=1 AND feature_039=1) is heterogeneous across racial/ethnic groups in feature_018 — specifically, the absolute gain in PFS is smaller in Black, Hispanic, and White patients than in Asian and Other patients.

analyses.result_summary: Within-race combo+ vs combo- mean PFS gaps: asian +1.44 mo (n=30), black +1.75 mo (n=58), hispanic +0.99 mo (n=78), other +2.81 mo (n=8), white +1.53 mo (n=355). In the interaction model (asian as ref): black -0.26 (p=0.0004), hispanic -0.23 (p=0.0009), white -0.19 (p=0.002), other +0.33 (p=0.01). Statistically significant heterogeneity, but the cell sizes for Asian and Other are very small so direction of point estimates is unstable.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The PFS benefit of the combo varies by insurance — privately insured and Medicare patients gain less from the combo than Medicaid (the reference).

analyses.result_summary: Combo×insurance interactions (medicaid as ref): medicare -0.118 (p=0.006), private -0.270 (p=4.6e-10), uninsured -0.030 (p=0.70). The combo's benefit appears largest among Medicaid patients and attenuated in private payers, though small subgroup sizes warrant caution.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The combo benefit on pfs_months interacts with disease severity feature_057: the benefit is smaller in higher-severity patients (feature_057=2) than in feature_057=0 baseline.

analyses.result_summary: Combo×feature_057 interactions: level 1 -0.116 mo (p=0.0002), level 2 +0.033 mo (p=0.44). Direction is mixed — combo benefit is attenuated at level 1 but indistinguishable from level 0 at level 2. Heterogeneity exists at level 1 but does not show a monotone severity gradient.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The combo benefit on pfs_months declines with higher feature_078 (i.e., negative combo×feature_078 interaction).

analyses.result_summary: Combo×feature_078 interaction coef = -0.0069 mo per unit (p=4.2e-7). The combo's benefit is reduced by ~0.007 mo for each one-unit higher feature_078 — a real but modest attenuation given the unit scale.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The positive effect of feature_078 on pfs_months is attenuated in patients with higher feature_057 levels (significant feature_078×feature_057 interaction, with negative interaction coefficient at level 2).

analyses.result_summary: Interaction coefficients: feature_078×C(feature_057)[T.1] = -0.0058 (p~1e-62); ×C(feature_057)[T.2] = -0.0213 (p<1e-300). The slope of feature_078 on PFS is markedly flatter at higher feature_057 levels — clinically, patients in the worst severity stratum have less benefit from elevated feature_078.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_078 and feature_013 interact: at higher log1p(feature_013), the positive slope of feature_078 on PFS is reduced.

analyses.result_summary: Interaction coef = -0.00339 mo per unit×unit (p~3.6e-151). High-feature_013 patients gain less per unit of feature_078; the two adverse/favorable axes partly cancel.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_057 modifies the effect of feature_013: the negative effect of higher feature_013 on PFS is dampened (less negative) at higher feature_057 levels.

analyses.result_summary: Interaction coefs: C(feature_057)[T.1]:log1p(feature_013) = +0.0135 (p=2e-6); [T.2] = +0.0517 (p~2e-38). The detrimental slope of feature_013 is attenuated at feature_057 level 2 (already poor prognosis — less remaining variance).

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_009 and feature_078 interact synergistically: the positive effect of feature_009 is greater at higher feature_078 (positive interaction).

analyses.result_summary: feature_009×feature_078 interaction coef = +0.0043 mo per unit×unit (p~5.5e-42). Confirms positive synergy: PFS benefit per unit of feature_009 grows by ~0.004 mo for each unit of feature_078.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The other multi-level ordinal features (feature_026 [0-10], feature_016 [0-4], feature_071, feature_096, feature_033, feature_064 [each 0-4], and feature_005 [6-22]) show no clinically meaningful gradient with pfs_months.

analyses.code: for c in those_cols: df.groupby(c).pfs_months.mean(); spearmanr(c, pfs_months)
analyses.result_summary: Group means span at most 0.16 mo across all ordinal levels for any of these features, with no monotone trend; |spearman rho| < 0.013 for each. None demonstrate a meaningful association with PFS.

proposed_hypotheses.text: After adjusting for clinical features and demographics, feature_027 (a moderate-prevalence binary, ~60%) has a small but real positive effect on pfs_months (~+0.025 mo).

analyses.result_summary: Adjusted feature_027 coef = +0.025 mo (p~1e-14). Real but small in magnitude; lower in priority than feature_109/feature_039 combo. Other binaries (feature_043, feature_121, feature_058) reach p<0.05 but with |coef|<0.026 mo and limited clinical importance.

proposed_hypotheses.text: A parsimonious final model with feature_078, C(feature_057), log1p(feature_013), feature_006, feature_009, feature_067, feature_109, feature_039, and the feature_109×feature_039 interaction explains the great majority of variance in pfs_months — and adding race or insurance does not improve the fit.

analyses.code: smf.ols('pfs_months ~ feature_078 + C(feature_057) + np.log1p(feature_013) + feature_006 + feature_009 + feature_067 + feature_109*feature_039', df).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Final model adj R^2 = 0.974. All clinical terms remain highly significant (|t|>7 except feature_067 t=-7.1) and the feature_109×feature_039 interaction stays at +1.43 mo (p<1e-300). Adding C(feature_018) or C(feature_045) yields ΔR^2 < 0.0001 and all demographic indicators p>0.66. Conclusion: PFS in this cohort is driven by a small set of clinical/biomarker variables and a single binary-feature combination; race and insurance carry no additional prognostic information after adjustment.
